title: Secrets and environments seem to behave differently
body:
### Issue Description

## Issue

Passing secrets to `podman run` as `--secret mysecretname,type=env,target=AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` Vs `-e AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=$AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` behaves differently. Authenticating with AWS works when the data is passed as an environment variable, but fails when passed as a secret.

The documented failure was reproduced with

- AWS S3 and the https://hub.docker.com/r/bitnami/aws-cli image
- Backblaze S3 storate and the ghcr.io/restic/restic image


### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Create a bucket in AWS or any S3-compatible solution. Here, I'll use backblaze.
2. Set the environment variables   

   ```
   export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=003d3e81b0748f50000000005
   export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<redacted>
   export RESTIC_PASSWORD=<redacted>
   ```
3. Set the secrets from the environment variables

   ```
   echo $AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID | podman secret create restic_backblaze_aws_access_key_id -
   echo $AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY | podman secret create restic_backblaze_aws_secret_access_key -
   echo $RESTIC_PASSWORD | podman secret create restic_password -
   ```
5. Use `restic init` with secrets ➡ this fails ❌ 

   ```
   podman run --rm --name=restic --secret restic_backblaze_aws_access_key_id,type=env,target=AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID --secret restic_backblaze_aws_secret_access_key,type=env,target=AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY --secret restic_password,type=env,target=RESTIC_PASSWORD -e RESTIC_REPOSITORY="s3:s3.eu-central-003.backblazeb2.com/templom-9-backups" restic -r s3:s3.eu-central-003.backblazeb2.com/templom-9-backups init
   ```
  
   the error message is `Fatal: create repository at s3:s3.eu-central-003.backblazeb2.com/templom-9-backups failed: Fatal: unable to open repository at s3:s3.eu-central-003.backblazeb2.com/templom-9-backups: client.BucketExists: Get "https://s3.eu-central-003.backblazeb2.com/templom-9-backups/?location=": net/http: invalid header field value for "Authorization"`

6. Use `restic init` with environment variables ➡ this works ✅  

   ```
   podman run --rm --name=restic -e AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=$AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID -e AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=$AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY -e RESTIC_PASSWORD=$RESTIC_PASSWORD -e RESTIC_REPOSITORY="s3:s3.eu-central-003.backblazeb2.com/templom-9-backups"  -v /mnt/raid1/immich/library/:/media/immich ghcr.io/restic/restic -r s3:s3.eu-central-003.backblazeb2.com/templom-9-backups init
   ```

---

When using the `aws-cli` image, the failing command returns:

```
podman run --rm --name=awscli --secret restic_backblaze_aws_access_key_id,type=env,target=AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID --secret restic_backblaze_aws_secret_access_key,type=env,target=AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY aws-cli -- s3 ls

An HTTP Client raised an unhandled exception: Invalid header value b'AWS4-HMAC-SHA256 Credential=AKIAWTR4VANA3NICSPF3\n/20250128/us-east-1/s3/aws4_request, SignedHeaders=host;x-amz-content-sha256;x-amz-date, Signature=559c1f06eb62519bcebf7467ef94406445f1363ad0b21c026b80dae22e3ecbb7'
```

### Describe the results you received

`Fatal: create repository at s3:s3.eu-central-003.backblazeb2.com/templom-9-backups failed: Fatal: unable to open repository at s3:s3.eu-central-003.backblazeb2.com/templom-9-backups: client.BucketExists: Get "https://s3.eu-central-003.backblazeb2.com/templom-9-backups/?location=": net/http: invalid header field value for "Authorization"`

---

When using the `aws-cli` image, the failing command returns:

```
podman run --rm --name=awscli --secret restic_backblaze_aws_access_key_id,type=env,target=AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID --secret restic_backblaze_aws_secret_access_key,type=env,target=AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY aws-cli -- s3 ls

An HTTP Client raised an unhandled exception: Invalid header value b'AWS4-HMAC-SHA256 Credential=AKIAWTR4VANA3NICSPF3\n/20250128/us-east-1/s3/aws4_request, SignedHeaders=host;x-amz-content-sha256;x-amz-date, Signature=559c1f06eb62519bcebf7467ef94406445f1363ad0b21c026b80dae22e3ecbb7'
```

### Describe the results you expected

The command with `--secret` should succeed.

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

I looked at the set environment variabled within the containers using commands like `podman run --rm --name=restic --secret restic_backblaze_aws_access_key_id,type=env,target=AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID --secret restic_backblaze_aws_secret_access_key,type=env,target=AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY --secret restic_password,type=env,target=RESTIC_PASSWORD  -v /mnt/raid1/immich/library/:/media/immich --entrypoint=/bin/sh restic -c 'echo $AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID'` and 👀 checked  the value of the environment variable in the container. I did not check for bytecoding differences or invisible characters. The values seem to be okay.

## Hypothesis

The `aws-cli` output (shared above) shows a `\n` as part of the credential. That might be an issue, and it was likely added by podman. ❓

comments.body:
Note we only support the latest release upstream. So you should test this with the latest version.
Also it would help a lot if you can provide an actual simple reproducer that anyone can just run, i.e. use a signle env/secret and dump the output in both cases

> The aws-cli output (shared above) shows a \n as part of the credential. That might be an issue, and it was likely added by podman. ❓


That would most likely be the issue then.
`echo $AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID | podman secret create restic_backblaze_aws_access_key_id -`

echo will always add a new line so that is most likely an issue with your commands not podman.

What you should do instead is to tell podman to read the value from the env instead of piping it https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-secret-create.1.html#env-false
`podman secret create --env restic_backblaze_aws_access_key_id AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`

Or try to use `echo -n`
`echo -n` did the trick. Thanks you, and sorry for the noise!
